name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَآءً وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٨)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion, a light and reminder for the Godwary (48)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ (٤٩)
```

```english
—those who fear their Lord in secret, and who are apprehensive of the Hour. (49)
```

```arabic
وَهَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ۚ أَفَأَنتُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ (٥٠)
```

```english
This [too] is a blessed reminder, which We have sent down. Will you then deny it? (50)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ رُشْدَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِۦ عَـٰلِمِينَ (٥١)
```

```english
Certainly We had given Abraham his probity before, and We knew him (51)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلتَّمَاثِيلُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَـٰكِفُونَ (٥٢)
```

```english
when he said to his father and his people, ‘What are these images to which you keep on clinging?’ (52)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا لَهَا عَـٰبِدِينَ (٥٣)
```

```english
They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them.’ (53)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنتُمْ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٥٤)
```

```english
He said, ‘Certainly you and your fathers have been in manifest error.’ (54)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱللَّـٰعِبِينَ (٥٥)
```

```english
They said, ‘Are you telling the truth, or are you [just] kidding?’ (55)
```

```arabic
قَالَ بَل رَّبُّكُمْ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا۠ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘Indeed, your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who originated them, and I affirm that. (56)
```

```arabic
وَتَٱللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنَـٰمَكُم بَعْدَ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٥٧)
```

```english
By Allah, I will devise a stratagem against your idols after you have gone away.’ (57)
```

```arabic
فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَٰذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَّهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
So he broke them into pieces—all except the biggest of them—so that they might come back to it. (58)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ مَن فَعَلَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَآ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٩)
```

```english
They said, ‘Whoever has done this to Our gods?! He is indeed a wrongdoer!’ (59)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقَالُ لَهُۥٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ (٦٠)
```

```english
They said, ‘We heard a young man mentioning them. He is called ‘‘Abraham.’’ ’ (60)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ فَأْتُوا۟ بِهِۦ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْيُنِ ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
They said, ‘Bring him before the people’s eyes so that they may bear witness [against him].’ (61)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَأَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَا يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ (٦٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?’ (62)
```

```arabic
قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُۥ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَـٰذَا فَسْـَٔلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ يَنطِقُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘No, it was this biggest one of them who did it! Ask them, if they can speak.’ (63)
```

```arabic
فَرَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ أَنتُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Thereat they came to themselves and said [to one another], ‘Indeed it is you who are the wrongdoers!’ (64)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رُءُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يَنطِقُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
Then they hung their heads. [However, they said,] ‘You certainly know that they cannot speak.’ (65)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ (٦٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘Then, do you worship besides Allah that which cannot cause you any benefit or harm? (66)
```

```arabic
أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
Fie on you and what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your reason?’ (67)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ حَرِّقُوهُ وَٱنصُرُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ (٦٨)
```

```english
They said, ‘Burn him, and help your gods, if you are to do anything!’ (68)
```

```arabic
قُلْنَا يَـٰنَارُ كُونِى بَرْدًا وَسَلَـٰمًا عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٦٩)
```

```english
We said, ‘O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!’ (69)
```

```arabic
وَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ (٧٠)
```

```english
They plotted to harm him, but We made them the biggest losers. (70)
```

```arabic
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧١)
```

```english
We delivered him and Lot toward the land, which We have blessed for all nations. (71)
```

```arabic
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَـٰلِحِينَ (٧٢)
```

```english
And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well for a grandson, and each of them We made righteous. (72)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَإِقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا عَـٰبِدِينَ (٧٣)
```

```english
We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them [concerning] the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayers, and the giving of zakat, and they used to worship Us. (73)
```

```arabic
وَلُوطًا ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَت تَّعْمَلُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ ۗ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَـٰسِقِينَ (٧٤)
```

```english
We gave judgement and knowledge to Lot, and We delivered him from the town which used to commit vicious acts. Indeed, they were an evil and depraved lot. (74)
```

```arabic
وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٧٥)
```

```english
And We admitted him into Our mercy. Indeed, he was one of the righteous. (75)
```

```arabic
وَنُوحًا إِذْ نَادَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٦)
```

```english
And before that Noah, when he called out, We responded to him and delivered him and his family from the great agony. (76)
```

```arabic
وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٧٧)
```

```english
And We helped him against the people who denied Our signs. They were indeed an evil lot; so We drowned them all. (77)
```

## (Commentary)

Having completed the discourse on prophethood (النبوة) by basing it upon the Return (المعاد), the author follows it by referring to the stories of a group of His noble prophets whom He sent to the people, aided with wisdom (الحكمة) and the law (الشريعة), and delivered from the hands of the wrongdoers (الظالمين) among their nations. In this, there is support for the argument of legislation (حجة التشريع) mentioned in the previous verses, as well as a warning and intimidation for the polytheists (المشركين) and glad tidings for the believers (المؤمنين).

He enumerates seventeen prophets therein: Moses, Aaron, Abraham, Lot, Isaac, Jacob, Noah, David, Solomon, Job, Ishmael, Idris, Dhul-Kifl, Dhul-Nun, Zechariah, John, and Jesus. In the verses currently under discussion, seven of them are mentioned. He mentions first Moses and Aaron, followed by Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Lot—who were before them—and then follows them with Noah, who was before all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ الْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَاءً وَذِكْرًا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** "Certainly We gave Moses and Aaron the Criterion, a light and a reminder for the God-conscious" (21:48) is a return, in a sense, to the detailing of what was summarized in His previous saying: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ** "We did not send [any apostles] before you except as men to whom We revealed..." (21:7). This is done by mentioning the gnosis (المعارف) and laws (الشرائع) given to the prophets, and how they were aided by the destruction of their enemies through the decree of justice (القضاء بالقسط).

The following verse testifies that what is meant by the Criterion (الفرقان), the light (الضياء), and the reminder (الذكر) is the Torah (التوراة), which Allah gave to Moses and his brother Aaron, his partner in prophethood.

The **Criterion** (الفرقان) — *the decisive standard that distinguishes truth from falsehood* — is a verbal noun like *al-farq* (الفرق), but it is more intensive. Al-Raghib mentioned that it is said to be a proper noun rather than a verbal noun. The Torah is named "the Criterion" because it distinguishes, or because through it, truth is distinguished from falsehood in belief and action. This verse is similar to His saying: **وَإِذْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ وَالْفُرْقَانَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ** "And when We gave Moses the Book and the Criterion so that you might be guided" (2:53). It is named "light" (ضياء) because it illuminates their path toward felicity (السعادة) and success (الفلاح) in this world and the Hereafter. It is named "a reminder" (ذكراً) because it contains the wisdom (الحكم), exhortations (المواعظ), and lessons (العبر) by which Allah is remembered.

Perhaps the fact that "the Criterion" is one of the names of the Torah is what necessitated its mention with the definite article (ال), unlike "light" and "reminder." From another perspective, it is a criterion for everyone, but it is a light and a reminder specifically for the God-conscious (المتقين), as others do not benefit from it. Therefore, "light" and "reminder" were mentioned in the indefinite form to be restricted by His saying: "for the God-conscious," unlike "the Criterion." The Torah has also been called "light" and "reminder" in His saying, the Exalted: **فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ** "...wherein was guidance and light" (5:44) and His saying: **فَاسْأَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ** "So ask the people of the Reminder..." (21:7).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهَٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُبَارَكٌ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ ۚ أَفَأَنْتُمْ لَهُ مُنْكِرُونَ** "And this is a blessed reminder which We have sent down. Will you then deny it?" (21:50). The demonstrative "this" (هذا) refers to the Quran. It is called a "blessed reminder" (ذكر مبارك) because it is firm, enduring, and abundant in blessings (البركات). Both the believer and the disbeliever in human society benefit from it, and the world enjoys its grace whether it recognizes it or denies it, and whether it acknowledges its truth or rejects it.

This is evidenced by analyzing the traces of maturity (الرشد) and righteousness (الصلاح) observed today in general human society and tracing them back to the era of the Quran's revelation and before. It is the blessed reminder whose meaning guides even if the ignorant are unaware of its wording, and even if the rejecters deny its right and are ungrateful for its great favor—aided in this by the Muslims through their neglect of its affair: **وَقَالَ الرَّسُولُ يَا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُورًا** "And the Apostle will say, ‘O my Lord! Indeed my people consigned this Quran to oblivion’" (25:30).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ رُشْدَهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِ عَالِمِينَ** "Certainly We gave Abraham his rectitude before, and We knew him well" (21:51). This is a turn back to the time before Moses, Aaron, and the revelation of the Torah, as indicated by His saying: "before" (من قبل). The meaning is that the giving of the Torah to Moses and Aaron was not an unprecedented matter; rather, I swear that We gave Abraham his rectitude before that.

**Rectitude** (الرشد) — *the innate capacity to perceive and follow the truth* — is the opposite of error (الغي), and it means hitting upon reality. In the case of Abraham (peace be upon him), it refers to his perfect, primordial guidance (اهتداؤه الفطري) toward monotheism (التوحيد) and all other true gnoses. The annexation of "rectitude" to the pronoun referring to Abraham implies exclusivity and conveys the sense of worthiness. This is supported by His subsequent saying: "and We knew him well," which is an allusion (كناية) to the knowledge of the particularity of his state and the extent of his potential.

The meaning is: I swear We gave Abraham the rectitude and attainment of reality for which he was prepared and of which he was worthy, and We were aware of the extent of his potential and merit. That which Allah, the Glorified, gave him—as previously mentioned—is what he perceived through the purity of his primordial nature (الفطرة) and the light of his insight (البصيرة) regarding the reality of monotheism and other true gnoses, without the instruction of a teacher, the reminder of a prompter, or the tutelage of an instructor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا هَٰذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنْتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ** "When he said to his father and his people, ‘What are these images to which you keep on clinging?’" (21:52). The **image** (التمثال) is a fashioned thing, and its plural is *tamathil* (تماثيل). **Clinging** (العكوف) — *the act of devotedly remaining in a place or state* — is to turn toward something and remain constant with it by way of veneration, as mentioned by Al-Raghib regarding both terms.

By these "images," he (peace be upon him) meant the idols they had set up for worship and the offering of sacrifices. His question about their reality was to identify their nature, as he had just entered society—a religious society that worshipped images and idols. The question, moreover, comprises two distinct inquiries. His questioning of his father regarding the idols occurred before his questioning of his people, as alluded to in Surah al-An'am. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ** "They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them’" (21:53). This is the response of the people. Since his (peace be upon him) question about the reality of the idols was essentially an inquiry into the reason for their worship, they clung to the tail of national custom in their justification, mentioning that it was the tradition of their fathers whom they found worshipping them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنْتُمْ أَنْتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُبِينٍ** "He said, ‘Certainly you and your fathers have been in manifest error’" (21:54). The reason for their being in manifest error (ضلال مبين) is what he would later present in his argument with the people after breaking the idols, in his saying: **أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنْفَعُكُمْ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ** "He said, ‘Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in any way, nor can it harm you?’" (21:66).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا أَجِئْتَنَا بِالْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنْتَ مِنَ اللَّاعِبِينَ** "They said, ‘Have you brought us the truth, or are you one of those who play?’" (21:55). This is a question of amazement and incredulity. This is the habit of the blind follower who acts without insight; when he encounters a denial of what he is engaged in, he finds it far-fetched and can hardly believe that it is something a denier could possibly deny. Thus, they asked him: "Have you brought us the truth, or are you one of those who play?" What is meant by "the truth" (الحق)—as indicated by the context—is seriousness; that is, "Do you say what you say in earnest, or are you playing with it?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ بَلْ رَبُّكُمْ رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الَّذِي فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ مِنَ الشَّاهِدِينَ** "He said, ‘Rather your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who originated them, and I am a witness to that’" (21:56). He (peace be upon him)—as you see—judges that their Lord is the Lord of the heavens, and that this Lord is the one who originated (فطر) the heavens and the earth, and He is Allah, the Glorified. In this, there is a complete opposition to their doctrine regarding Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية). They believed they had a god or gods other than the god or gods of the heavens and the earth, and they did not consider Allah, the Glorified, to be their god or the god of anything in the heavens and the earth; rather, they believed He is the God of gods, the Lord of lords, and the Originator of all.

Thus, his saying: "Rather your Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, who originated them" is a rejection of their doctrine of divinity in all its aspects and an affirmation that there is no god but Allah, which is monotheism (التوحيد).

Then he (peace be upon him) revealed by his saying: "and I am a witness to that" that he acknowledges and affirms what he said, committed to its implications and effects, witnessing it with a testimony of affirmation and commitment. For knowledge of a thing is different from commitment to it, and they may sometimes diverge, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنْفُسُهُمْ** "They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts..." (27:14).

With this testimony, the answer to their question—whether he is serious in what he says or playing—is completed. The answer is: No, rather I know this and I profess it as my religion.

This is what the context provides regarding the meaning of the verse. Others have different opinions in its interpretation, as well as other views regarding the meanings of the preceding and succeeding verses of the story, which we have set aside due to a lack of benefit in addressing them, as neither the context of the verses supports them nor do the doctrines of idolatry (الوثنية) agree with them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَتَاللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنامَكُمْ بَعْدَ أَنْ تُوَلُّوا مُدْبِرِينَ** "By Allah, I will surely devise a stratagem against your idols after you have turned your backs" (21:57). This is coordinated with his saying: "Rather your Lord..." etc., meaning: he said, "I will surely devise a stratagem against your idols," etc. **Stratagem** (الكيد) — *a hidden plan or plot intended to cause harm or a desired outcome* — is a subtle management against something to its detriment. In His saying: "after you have turned your backs," there is an indication that they used to leave the city or the house of idols occasionally for a festival they had or the like, leaving the atmosphere empty.

The context of the story and the nature of this speech require that his saying: "By Allah, I will surely devise a stratagem against your idols" be understood as his firm resolve to plot against their idols. Often, firm resolve is expressed as "saying"; it is said, "I will do such-and-such because of a word I said," meaning because of a resolve I made.

It is unlikely that he was addressing the people with this, as they were a large idolatrous nation possessing power, prestige, fervor, and zealotry, and there was no monotheist among them at that time—it being the beginning of Abraham's call—except him. It would not have been prudent to inform the people of his intention to harm their idols, especially by explicitly stating that it would be through a stratagem on a day when the city or the house of idols was empty of people, like one who divulges a secret to the very person from whom he wishes to hide it. This is unless he was addressing some of the people from whom the word would not spread; as for announcing the secret to the general public, it was certainly not so.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَاذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ** "So he smashed them into pieces, except the biggest of them, so that they might come back to it" (21:58). Al-Raghib said: **Smashing** (الجذ) is to break and crumble something. It is said of broken gold stones and gold crumbs *judhadh* (جذاذاً), and from this is His saying: "So he smashed them into pieces." End quote. Thus, the meaning is that he made the idols into broken pieces, except for one large idol among them.

Regarding His saying: "so that they might come back to it," the apparent context is that this hope (الترجي) is to explain what his action represented. That is, his action—breaking all but one large idol—was the act of one who intends for the people to see what had befallen their idols of smashing, find their "great one" intact among them, and then return to it and accuse it in their affair, like one who kills a group of people and leaves one of them so that he may be accused in their affair.

Based on this, the pronoun in His saying: "to it" (إليه) refers to "the biggest of them" (كبيراً لهم). This meaning is also supported by Abraham's subsequent saying: "Rather it was this biggest of them who did it," in response to their saying: "Was it you who did this to our gods?"

The majority of commentators are of the view that the pronoun in "to it" refers to Abraham (peace be upon him). The meaning would be: he broke the idols and left their biggest one so that the people might return to Abraham, and he could then argue with them, silence them, and demonstrate the falsehood of their idols' divinity. Some held that the pronoun refers to Allah, the Glorified, meaning: he broke them and left it so that the people might return to Allah in worship when they saw the state of the idols and realized from their breaking that they were not gods as they had claimed.

It is not hidden that both views necessitate that His saying: "except the biggest of them" be redundant (مستدركاً), even if some have labored to refute this with arguments that avail nothing. What prevented them from referring the pronoun to "the biggest" was the perceived lack of soundness of the "hope" (الترجي) on this assumption. However, you have realized that this is to explain what his (peace be upon him) action represented to whoever witnessed the scene, not to express a serious hope from Abraham (peace be upon him).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا مَنْ فَعَلَ هَٰذَا بِآلِهَتِنَا إِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** "They said, ‘Whoever has done this to our gods? He is indeed one of the wrongdoers!’" (21:59). This is an inquiry motivated by regret and a desire to investigate the matter to find the perpetrator who committed the wrongdoing. This is supported by the following saying: "They said, ‘We heard a youth speaking of them...’" etc. Thus, the view of some that "whoever" (من) is a relative pronoun is not sound.

His saying: "He is indeed one of the wrongdoers" is a judgment from them that he is an unjust person who must be punished for his wrongdoing. For he wronged the gods by transgressing against their right, which is veneration; he wronged the people by transgressing against their right, which is the respect of their gods and the sanctity of their sacred things; and he wronged himself by transgressing into what was not his right and committing what he had no business committing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا سَمِعْنَا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقَالُ لَهُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ** "They said, ‘We heard a youth speaking of them, who is called Abraham’" (21:60). What is meant by "speaking" (الذكر)—as understood from the context—is speaking ill; that is, "We heard a youth speaking ill of the gods." If so, then he is the one who did this to them, for no one would dare to commit such a crime except one who is so audacious.

His saying: "who is called Abraham" (يُقَالُ لَهُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ) with *Ibrahim* in the nominative case is the predicate of a deleted subject, the estimation being "He is Abraham," as mentioned by Al-Zamakhshari.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا فَأْتُوا بِهِ عَلَىٰ أَعْيُنِ النَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ** "They said, ‘Then bring him before the people’s eyes so that they may bear witness’" (21:61). What is meant by bringing him "before the people's eyes" is to produce him in a gathering of people and in their sight, which was where the idols were broken, as appears from Abraham's (peace be upon him) saying: "Rather it was this biggest of them who did it," while pointing to the biggest of the idols.

It seems that what is meant by their "bearing witness" is that they should testify against him that he used to speak ill of them, which would be a pretext to extract a confession from him regarding the smashing and breaking. As for the view that what is meant is their witnessing the punishment of Abraham for what he did, it is far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا أَأَنْتَ فَعَلْتَ هَٰذَا بِآلِهَتِنَا يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ** "They said, ‘Was it you who did this to our gods, O Abraham?’" (21:62). The inquiry—as has been said—is for the purpose of obtaining an admission from the perpetrator, for the act itself is taken for granted and its occurrence is known. In their saying "to our gods," there is an allusion that they did not count him among the idol-worshippers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَٰذَا فَاسْأَلُوهُمْ إِنْ كَانُوا يَنْطِقُونَ** "He said, ‘Rather it was this biggest of them who did it! Ask them, if they can speak’" (21:63). What he (peace be upon him) reported in his saying: "Rather it was this biggest of them who did it" is a claim made for the purpose of silencing the opponent and a hypothetical assumption intended to invalidate their divinity, as he would explicitly state in his saying: "Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in any way, nor can it harm you?" etc. It is not a serious report at all. This is frequently found in disputes and debates.

Thus, the meaning is: He said, "Rather, the evidence of the situation—which is the fact that all have become smashed pieces while their biggest remains intact—testifies that this biggest of them did it." This is a preparation for his saying: "Ask them," etc.

His saying: "Ask them, if they can speak" is a command to ask the idols about the reality of the situation and who it was that did this to them, so they might inform them if they can speak. Thus, His saying: "if they can speak" is a condition whose apodosis is deleted, indicated by His saying: "Ask them."

In conclusion, the verse is understood according to its literal meaning without the burden of assuming hidden words, transposition, or the pitfall of convolution. Its beginning, which contains the claim of attributing the act to their biggest idol, is to silence the opponent and serve as a preamble for its end—which is the command to ask the idols if they speak—so that it leads to the people's admission that they do not speak.

It has been said that His saying: "if they can speak" is a qualification for His saying: "Rather it was this biggest of them who did it," the estimation being: "Rather, if they can speak, then their biggest did it." Since their speaking is impossible, the act from it is likewise impossible, and His saying: "Ask them" is a parenthetical sentence.

It has also been said that the subject of His saying: "did it" (فعله) is deleted, the estimation being: "Rather, he who did it, did it," then he began anew: "this biggest of them, so ask them," etc. Other views have also been mentioned, but they are not free from artificiality, and the speech would then suffer from a convolution from which the speech of the Exalted is transcendent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَرَجَعُوا إِلَىٰ أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوا إِنَّكُمْ أَنْتُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** "Thereat they came to themselves and said [to one another], ‘Indeed it is you who are the wrongdoers’" (21:64). This is a consequence of his saying: "Ask them, if they can speak." For when they heard that from him, while seeing that the idols were inanimate objects possessing neither consciousness nor speech, the argument was established against them. Each of them then judged against himself that he was the wrongdoer, not Abraham. Thus, His saying: "they came to themselves" is a metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية) to their realization and their reflection within themselves. His saying: "and said, ‘Indeed it is you who are the wrongdoers’" means each said to his own soul, addressing it: "Indeed, you are the wrongdoer for worshipping an inanimate object that does not speak."

It was said: The meaning is that they turned to one another and said to one another, "Indeed, it is you who are the wrongdoers." But you are aware that this does not suit the context, which is a situation where the argument has been completed against everyone and they share in the wrongdoing. If it were based on what they said to one another in a situation of this nature, it would have been more appropriate to say: "Indeed, we are the wrongdoers," as in similar instances. He, the Exalted, said: **فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَلَاوَمُونَ قَالُوا يَا وَيْلَنَا إِنَّا كُنَّا طَاغِينَ** "Then they turned to one another, blaming each other. They said, ‘Woe to us! We were indeed rebellious’" (68:30-31), and He said: **فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ** "...and you would be left to wonder: ‘We are indeed debt-ridden! Rather, we are deprived’" (56:65-67).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا عَلَىٰ رُؤُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ يَنْطِقُونَ** "Then they relapsed into their [former] state: ‘You certainly know that these do not speak’" (21:65). Al-Raghib said: **Relapsing** (النكس) is to turn something on its head; from this is the "relapsed child" if his feet come out before his head. He, the Exalted, said: "Then they relapsed into their [former] state." End quote. Thus, His saying: "Then they relapsed into their [former] state" is an allusion or a metaphorical allusion to their turning falsehood into the place of the truth that had appeared to them, and truth into the place of falsehood. It is as if the truth had risen in their hearts over falsehood, so they relapsed, raising falsehood—which was that Abraham was a wrongdoer—over the truth, which was that they themselves were the wrongdoers. Thus, they countered Abraham by saying: "You certainly know that these do not speak."

The meaning of their saying: "You certainly know," etc., is that your defense of yourself by attributing the act—the smashing—to the biggest of the idols and conditioning that upon making the gods speak, while knowing they do not speak, is proof that you are the wrongdoing perpetrator. Thus, the sentence is an allusion to the establishment of the crime and a judgment against Abraham.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنْفَعُكُمْ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ** — to His saying — **أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** "He said, ‘Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in any way, nor can it harm you? Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah! Do you not exercise your reason?’" (21:66-67). When they uttered their saying: "these do not speak," and Abraham heard it, he did not occupy himself with defense, for that was not his primary aim. Rather, he utilized their words for his true call, silencing them with the implication of their own words and completing the argument against them regarding the fact that their idols were not worthy of worship—that is, they were not gods.

The result of the consequence in His saying: "Do you then worship besides Allah that which cannot benefit you in any way, nor can it harm you?" is that the necessary implication of their not speaking is that they know nothing and are capable of nothing. The necessary implication of that is that they cannot benefit you in any way nor harm you. The necessary implication of that is that worshipping them is futile, for worship is either out of hope for good or fear of evil, and they possess neither. Therefore, they are not gods.

His saying: "Fie on you and on what you worship besides Allah" is an expression of disgust and a disavowal of them and their gods after the invalidation of their divinity. This is like his testimony to His oneness, the Exalted, after proving it in his previous saying: "and I am a witness to that." His saying: "Do you not exercise your reason?" is a rebuke to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَانْصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فَاعِلِينَ** "They said, ‘Burn him, and help your gods, if you are to do [anything]!’" (21:68). Although he (peace be upon him) had invalidated the divinity of the idols with his previous speech—the implicit consequence of which was that their breaking was neither a wrongdoing nor a crime—he had alluded in his speech that his attributing the act to the biggest of the idols and commanding them to ask the gods about it was not to deflect the crime from himself, but rather a preamble to invalidate the divinity of the gods. With this amount of silence and lack of rebuttal, they judged against him that the crime was established and that his punishment was to be burned with fire.

Therefore, they said: "Burn him, and help your gods" by magnifying their affair and penalizing the one who insulted them. Their saying: "if you are to do [anything]" is an incitement and provocation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ** "We said, ‘O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!’" (21:69). This is an existential command (خطاب تكويني) to the fire, by which its properties of heat, burning, and annihilation were transformed into coolness and safety in relation to Abraham (peace be upon him) by way of a miracle (خرق العادة). From this, it becomes clear that we have no way to grasp the reality of the matter in detail, for rational inquiries into cosmic events only apply where we have knowledge of the causal relationships (روابط العلية والمعلولية) in repeated, habitual occurrences. As for miracles whose relationships we are ignorant of, they have no place therein. Yes, we know in general that the resolves of souls (همم النفوس) have a role in them, and we have discussed this in the discourses on miracles (الإعجاز) in the first volume of this book.

The disjunction (الفصل) in His saying: "We said," etc., is because it is in the sense of an answer to a presumed question. The estimation of the speech, with its deletions for the sake of conciseness, is something like: "So they kindled a fire and threw him into it." It was as if it were asked: "What happened after that?" and it was said: **قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا عَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ** (We said, ‘O fire! Be cool and safe for Abraham!’). In this manner, the disjunction occurs in every "he said" (قال) and "they said" (قالوا) in the previous verses of the story.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَرَادُوا بِهِ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَاهُمُ الْأَخْسَرِينَ** "They desired to outwit him, but We made them the greatest losers" (21:70). That is, they plotted against him to extinguish his light and invalidate his argument, but We made them the greatest losers (الأخسرين), as they lost through the invalidation of their plot and its lack of effect, and their loss increased when Allah made him prevail over them through protection and deliverance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ الَّتِي بَارَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَالَمِينَ** "And We delivered him and Lot to the land which We have blessed for all the worlds" (21:71). The mentioned land is the land of Sham (Syria/Levant), to which Abraham migrated. Lot was the first to believe in him and migrate with him, as He, the Exalted, said: **فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ ۘ وَقَالَ إِنِّي مُهاجِرٌ إِلى رَبِّي** "So Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘Indeed I am migrating toward my Lord’" (29:26).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً** "And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well" (21:72). The **gift** (النافلة) — *an additional or voluntary bestowal beyond what is expected* — is a boon. The discussion on the content of these two verses has been repeated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا** "We made them imams, guiding by Our command..." (21:73) to the end of the verse. It appears—as indicated by the verses (1) that point to the placement of the Imamate (الإمامة) in the progeny of Abraham (peace be upon him)—that the pronoun in "We made them" refers to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

The apparent meaning of His saying: "imams, guiding by Our command" is that "guiding by the command" serves as an explanation for the meaning of Imamate. We have previously discussed the meaning of the Imam's guidance by the command of Allah in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنِّي جَاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِمَامًا** "‘I am making you an imam of mankind’" (2:124) in the first volume of this book.

What pertains specifically to this context is that this guidance, which is made one of the functions of the Imamate, is not in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق), because Allah, the Glorified, made Abraham (peace be upon him) an Imam after He had made him a Prophet—as we clarified in the interpretation of His saying: "I am making you an imam of mankind" previously. Prophethood (النبوة) is never inseparable from guidance in the sense of showing the way. Thus, nothing remains for the Imamate except guidance in the sense of "conveying to the goal" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب), which is a type of existential disposal (تصرف تكويني) over souls by steering them in the journey of perfection and moving them from one spiritual station to another.

Since it is an existential disposal and an inward act (عمل باطني), what is meant by the "command" (الأمر) by which the guidance occurs is not the legislative, conventional command (الأمر التشريعي الاعتباري), but rather what He explains in His saying: **إِنَّمَا أَمْرُهُ إِذَا أَرَادَ شَيْئًا أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحَانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things..." (36:82-83). It refers to the spiritual outpourings (الفيوضات المعنوية) and inward stations (المقامات الباطنية) to which the believers are guided by their righteous deeds (أعمالهم الصالحة) and with which they are clothed as a mercy from their Lord.

Since the Imam guides by the command—and the particle *ba* (بـ) denotes causality or instrumentality—he is clothed with it first, and from him it spreads among the people according to their different stations. Thus, the Imam is the link between the people and their Lord in the giving and receiving of inward outpourings, just as the Prophet is the link between the people and their Lord in receiving outward outpourings, which are the divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية) that descend by revelation (الوحي) upon the Prophet and spread from him and through his mediation to and among the people. The Imam is a guide who leads souls to their stations, just as the Prophet is a guide who leads people to true beliefs and righteous deeds. Sometimes, prophethood and Imamate are combined, as in Abraham and his two sons.

__________________
(1) Such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةً بَاقِيَةً فِي عَقِبِهِ** "He made it a lasting word among his posterity..." (43:28) and others.

His saying: **وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ** "and We revealed to them the doing of good works, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat" (21:73). The annexation of the verbal noun (المصدر) to its object implies the actualization of its meaning in external reality. If it were intended not to imply this, the annexation would be severed, or the particles *an* (أن) and *anna* (أنّ)—which indicate the interpretation of the verbal noun—would be used; Al-Jurjani explicitly stated this in *Dala'il al-I'jaz*. Thus, our saying: "Your excellence and your doing good please me," and His saying, the Exalted: **مَا كَانَ اللَّهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمَانَكُمْ** "Allah would not let your faith go to waste" (2:143) indicates prior occurrence. Our saying: "It pleases me that you excel and that you do good," and His saying, the Exalted: **أَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ** "...that you fast is better for you" (2:184) does not indicate prior actualization. Therefore, it was customary in the verses of call and legislation to use *an* and the verb rather than the annexed verbal noun, such as His saying: **أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ** "I have been commanded to worship Allah" (13:36), and **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ** "...that you shall not worship anyone except Him" (12:40), and **وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ** "...and to maintain the prayer" (6:72).

Accordingly, His saying: "and We revealed to them the doing of good works," etc., indicates the actualization of the act. That is, the revelation pertained to the act proceeding from them; the act was proceeding from them by a revelation accompanying it and an inward divine guidance. This is different from the legislative revelation (الوحي المشرع) which legislates the act first, and the performance of the act follows according to what was legislated.

This is supported by His subsequent saying: **وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ** "and they used to be Our worshippers" (21:73), for it outwardly indicates that they were worshippers of Allah before that, and then they were aided by the revelation. Their worship of Allah was through acts legislated for them by the legislative revelation previously. Thus, this revelation pertaining to the doing of good works is a revelation of strengthening (وحي تسديد), not a revelation of legislation (وحي تشريع).

In summary, they were aided by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس) and purity, strengthened by a lordly power that called them to the doing of good works, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat—which was the specific monetary spending in their law.

The commentators interpreted the revelation in the verse as the legislative revelation, which caused them difficulties. First, from the perspective that "the doing of good works" in the sense of a verbal noun is not the object of revelation; rather, its object is the result of the act. Second, that legislation is general to the prophets and their nations, yet it was specified to them in the verse. Therefore, Al-Zamakhshari mentioned that what is meant by "the doing of good works" and what follows it is the passive verbal noun (المصدر المبني للمفعول), the meaning being: "and We revealed to them that good works be done"—in the passive voice—and so on. By this, the two difficulties are repelled: since the passive verbal noun and the result of the act are like synonyms, the first difficulty is repelled; and the subject therein is unknown, applying to the prophets and their nations together, thus repelling the second difficulty. Much discussion has surrounded what he mentioned.

In response: First, the claimed identity of meaning between the passive verbal noun and the result of the act is denied.

Second: What we previously stated, that the annexation of the verbal noun to its object implies the actualization of the act, and legislative revelation does not pertain to it.

The story of Abraham (peace be upon him) has already been presented in the commentary on Surah al-An'am, and the story of Jacob (peace be upon him) in the commentary on Surah Yusuf in this book. The story of Isaac will come in the commentary on Surah al-Saffat, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلُوطًا آتَيْنَاهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا** "And to Lot We gave judgement and knowledge..." (21:74) to the end of the two verses. **Judgement** (الحكم) is in the sense of resolving disputes or in the sense of wisdom (الحكمة). The town that used to commit abominations (الخبائث) was Sodom, where Lot settled during his migration with Abraham (peace be upon him). What is meant by "abominations" are the foul acts. What is meant by "mercy" (الرحمة) is either guardianship (الولاية) or prophethood (النبوة), and there is a basis for each. The story of Lot (peace be upon him) has already been presented in the commentary on Surah Hud in this book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنُوحًا إِذْ نَادَىٰ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَاسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُ** "And Noah, when he called out before, so We responded to him..." (21:76) to the end of the two verses. That is: and remember Noah when he called out to his Lord before Abraham and those mentioned with him, and We responded to him. His calling out is what the Glorified narrated of his saying: **فَدَعَا رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانْتَصِرْ** "So he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘I am overcome, so help [me]!’" (54:10). What is meant by his "family" (أهله) are his close ones, except for his wife and his drowned son. **Distress** (الكرب) is intense grief. His saying: **وَنَصَرْنَاهُ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ** "And We helped him against the people..." (21:77)—it is as if "help" (النصر) is imbued with the meaning of "deliverance" (الإنجاء) and the like, which is why it is followed by the preposition *min* (من). The rest is clear.

The story of Noah (peace be upon him) has already been presented in the commentary on Surah Hud in this book.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*: Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr, from Aban ibn Uthman, from Hujr, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Abraham (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) opposed his people and criticized their gods..." until his saying: "When they turned their backs on him to go to a festival of theirs, Abraham (ص) entered upon their gods with an ax. He smashed them except for a large one among them and placed the ax around its neck. When they returned to their gods and saw what had been done to them, they said: 'No, by Allah, no one would have dared against them or smashed them except the youth who used to criticize them and disavow them.' They found no way to kill him more severe than fire.

So they gathered wood for him and sought the best of it. When the day came on which he was to be burned, Nimrod and his soldiers came out, and a structure had been built for him so he could watch how the fire would take him. Abraham was placed in a catapult. The earth said: 'O Lord, there is no one on my back who worships You other than him, and he is being burned with fire?' The Lord said: 'If he calls upon Me, I shall suffice him.'"

Aban mentioned from Muhammad ibn Marwan, from one who narrated it from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "The supplication of Abraham (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on that day was: 'O One, O One, O Besought, O Besought—O He who neither begets nor is begotten, and there is none like unto Him.' Then he said: 'I have relied upon Allah.' The Lord, Blessed and Exalted, said: 'I have sufficed you.' He said to the fire: 'Be cool!' He said: 'Abraham's teeth chattered from the cold until Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: "and safe for Abraham." Gabriel descended, and there he was, sitting with Abraham, talking to him in the fire.'

Nimrod said: 'Whoever takes a god, let him take one like the God of Abraham.' He said: 'One of their grandees said: "I have commanded the fire not to burn him." A neck of fire reached out toward him and burned him.' He said: 'So Lot believed in him, and he went out as a migrant to Sham, he, Sarah, and Lot.'"

In the same source, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, and a number of our companions, from Sahl ibn Ziyad, all from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Ibrahim ibn Abi Ziyad al-Karkhi, who said: "I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) say: 'When Abraham (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) smashed the idols of Nimrod, Nimrod ordered him to be bound. He made an enclosure for him, gathered wood in it, and kindled the fire. Then he cast Abraham into the fire to burn him. They then withdrew from it until the fire died down. They then looked into the enclosure and there was Abraham, safe and released from his bonds.'

Nimrod was informed of his news and ordered that Abraham be expelled from his lands and prevented from leaving with his livestock and wealth. Abraham argued with them then, saying: 'If you take my livestock and wealth, then it is my right upon you that you return to me what has passed of my life in your lands.' They took their dispute to Nimrod's judge, who ruled against Abraham that he must surrender to them all that he had acquired in their lands, and he ruled against Nimrod's companions that they must return to Abraham what had passed of his life in his lands. Nimrod was informed of this and ordered them to let him, his livestock, and his wealth go, and to expel him. He said: 'If he remains in your lands, he will corrupt your religion and harm your gods.'" The tradition continues.

In *al-Ilal*, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Hilal, who said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'When Abraham (peace be upon him) was cast into the fire, Gabriel met him in the air as he was falling and said: "O Abraham, do you have a need?" He replied: "As for from you, then no."'"

**I say:** The tradition of his being cast by a catapult has been reported in several narrations from both the Sunnis and the Shi'a, as has Gabriel's saying to him: "Do you have a need?" and his reply: "As for from you, then no." Both groups have narrated it.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Faryabi, Ibn Abi Shaybah, and Ibn Jarir narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: **قُلْنَا يَا نَارُ كُونِي بَرْدًا** (We said, ‘O fire! Be cool’): "It became so cool for him that it almost harmed him, until it was said: **وَسَلَامًا** (and safe). He said: 'It did not harm him.'"

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Uyun*, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in a tradition regarding the Imamate (الإمامة), he said: "Then Allah, Mighty and Majestic, honored him—meaning Abraham—by placing it—meaning the Imamate—in his progeny and the people of choice and purity. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ** (And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well, and each of them We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the doing of good works, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers). It continued in his progeny, inherited from one to another, generation after generation, until the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) inherited it. Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, said: **إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَٰذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ۗ وَاللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith, and Allah is the guardian of the faithful). Thus, it was exclusive.

Then Ali (peace be upon him) was invested with it by the command of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, according to the pattern Allah, the Exalted, had ordained. It then became established in his chosen progeny to whom Allah gave knowledge and faith, according to His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمَانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ** (But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have stayed in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection’). Thus, it is specifically in the children of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) until the Day of Resurrection, for there is no prophet after Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain to Yahya ibn Umran, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً** (And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as well): "He said: 'The child of a child is a gift (نافلة).'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi* regarding His saying: **وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ مِنَ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كَانَتْ تَعْمَلُ الْخَبَائِثَ** (And We delivered him from the town that used to commit abominations): "He said: 'They used to practice sodomy.'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding the stories of Abraham (peace be upon him) are very numerous, but they differ greatly in details that do not pertain to the explicit text of the Book. We have sufficed with what we have presented. We have already provided what is extracted from his stories from His speech, the Exalted, in the commentary on Surah al-An'am in the seventh volume of this book.
